executive_summary.one_paragraph: CD81 is a canonical tetraspanin (4-TM, two extracellular loops) with high, direct-multi-method evidence for plasma-membrane surface accessibility across diverse human cell types. Live-cell flow cytometry on AML patient blasts (n=290) and multiple cell lines, surface biotinylation MS with >2-fold enrichment, immunogold EM on intact EVs, and biochemical topology mapping collectively confirm surface exposure of both ECL1 and the structurally defined LEL/ECL2. Expression is broadly moderate-to-high across hematolymphoid, neuroectodermal, and mesenchymal lineages, with disease-state upregulation in non-CBF AML LSCs and high-grade glioma. Consequential accessibility risks are modest: the LEL is small (~88 residues), an intracellular pool co-exists with the surface pool, and expression can be near-absent in some hepatoma lines at baseline.

surface_evidence.grade_rationale: CD81 surface accessibility is supported by multiple independent direct methods from multiple independent sources. (1) Live-cell flow cytometry (non-permeabilized) on HepG2-hCD81 overexpressors, HEK293T cells, and primary AML patient bone marrow blasts (n=290) confirms plasma-membrane exposure (a1_evi_11, a1_evi_12, a1_evi_13, a1_evi_16, a1_evi_17). (2) Surface biotinylation with membrane-impermeable Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin on live cells, followed by streptavidin enrichment and both WB and MS readout, identified CD81 with >2-fold enrichment across ≥2 independent biotinylation strategies (a1_evi_22, a1_evi_24, a1_evi_25, a1_evi_26). (3) Mass spectrometry surfaceome (a1_evi_25, a1_evi_26, a1_evi_31). (4) Immunogold EM on intact, non-permeabilized EVs (a1_evi_20). Topology is biochemically confirmed by microsomal proteolysis placing both hydrophilic loops extracellularly (a1_evi_03, a1_evi_04) and by LEL crystal structure (a1_evi_08). No contradictory evidence is present. Grade: direct_multi_method.

antibodies.name: anti-TAPA-1 antiproliferative antibody
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Original discovery antibody used in intact-cell immunoprecipitation; epitope mapped to second extracellular domain (LEL/ECL2).

expression_observations.context: Most human cell lines; intact-cell immunoprecipitation identified 26 kDa surface protein TAPA-1 (CD81)

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface flow cytometry)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Wild-type HepG2 (CD81-negative) used as negative control baseline; HepG2-overexpressing hCD81 used as positive control.

expression_observations.context: Wild-type HepG2 cells lack detectable surface CD81 (negative baseline)
HepG2 cells stably overexpressing human CD81 show high surface CD81 signal

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface flow cytometry, AML cohort)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Validated by correlation with CD81 mRNA levels (r²=0.51) across AML patient cohort.

expression_observations.context: AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis (n=290 total; n=226 non-CBF, n=26 CBF); surface CD81 quantified by flow cytometry histograms and violin plots
Hematogones (normal bone marrow precursors) as internal comparison population in AML bone marrow samples

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (surface flow cytometry, HEK293T cells and EVs)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Single-cell CRISPR knockout of CD81 (and other tetraspanins CD9, CD63) used as negative controls to validate antibody specificity.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T wild-type cells positive for CD81 on cell surface
Wild-type HEK293T-derived extracellular vesicles (WT-EVs) positive for CD81 surface expression

antibodies.name: anti-CD19 surface staining antibody (CD81 trafficking assay readout)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: CD81 co-transfection with CD19-APEX2; surface CD19 staining used as functional readout of CD81-mediated trafficking; confirms CD81 plasma membrane residency.

expression_observations.context: HEK293T cells co-transfected with CD19-APEX2 + CD81 show ~3-fold increase in cell surface staining vs CD19-APEX2 alone; confirms CD81 surface residency

antibodies.name: MACSPlex Exosome Kit antibodies (CD81 among 37 surface markers)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Bead-based multiplex assay; includes two isotype-control bead populations as internal controls. Simultaneous detection of 37 surface epitopes on EVs.

expression_observations.context: Small extracellular vesicles (sEVs) characterized for CD81 surface expression by bead-based flow cytometry (MACSPlex Exosome Kit)

antibodies.name: anti-CD9 / anti-CD81 immunogold EM antibodies
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Immunogold EM (iEM) performed on intact extracellular vesicles without permeabilization to detect surface-exposed CD9 and CD81.

expression_observations.context: Extracellular vesicles derived from cell lines; CD9 and CD81 detected on EV surface by immunogold EM

Glioblastoma cell lines (U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG) surface-biotinylated with membrane-impermeable EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (1 mg/mL, pH 8, 30 min) to label plasma-membrane-exposed proteins including CD81

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (Western blot validation, EV surface biotinylation)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used at 1:500 dilution overnight at 4°C to confirm CD81 presence in streptavidin-bead-enriched EV surface fraction by Western blot, paired with MS.

expression_observations.context: Medium and small extracellular vesicles (mEVs, sEVs) surface-biotinylated with EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (10 mM, pH 7.4) after ultracentrifugation; CD81 identified in surface-enriched fraction by WB and global/surface proteomics

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (Western blot validation post-streptavidin enrichment)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: WB used alongside MS to validate streptavidin-bead-enriched surface biotinylation eluants; CD81 detected with >2-fold enrichment by at least two independent biotinylation strategies.

expression_observations.context: Cancer cell lines (unspecified); CD81 identified among 9 proteins most enriched by surface biotinylation MS; >2-fold enrichment confirmed by at least two independent biotinylation strategies
HeLa cells and derived EVs; 136/456 (30%) proteins annotated as membrane/cell-surface including CD81; CD81 carries transmembrane domains confirmed by UniProt annotation

antibodies.name: rabbit anti-CD81
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: Used at 1:200 dilution in GBM tissue / PBMC immunofluorescence study. Permeabilization status not stated in the extracted quote.

expression_observations.context: Glioblastoma tissue and/or PBMCs; anti-CD81 (Abcam ab219209, 1:200) used in immunofluorescence panel alongside markers for immune and stromal cells

antibodies.name: anti-CD81 (HPA IHC)
antibodies.cross_reactivity_notes: HPA database annotation based on IHC evidence; plasma membrane localization flagged.

expression_observations.context: Human tissues profiled by HPA; CD81 annotated at plasma membrane and cytosol by IHC

non_surface_expression.context: HeLa cells — CD81 detected by Western blot in whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations as a loading/marker control; whole-cell WB does not distinguish surface vs. intracellular pool

tissues.tissue: multiple tissues / cell lines (hematolymphoid, neuroectodermal, mesenchymal)

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Broad human cell types under basal conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: Same cells; HPA annotation documents simultaneous plasma membrane and cytosolic pools
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 partitions between the plasma membrane and a cytosolic pool; the relative sizes of these pools may vary by cell type or state.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: Surface-accessible fraction co-exists with an intracellular pool; binder access is limited to the plasma membrane-resident fraction, which may not represent the total cellular CD81.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Normal hematogones (B-cell precursors) and hematopoietic cells in bone marrow
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis, particularly non-CBF AML; leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations
accessibility_modulation.change: CD81 surface expression is significantly elevated on AML blasts compared to normal hematogones, with highest levels in non-CBF AML and LSC subpopulations; CBF-AML (favorable prognosis) shows lower CD81 surface and mRNA levels.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: High CD81 surface density on AML blasts — especially in aggressive non-CBF and LSC-enriched subpopulations — creates a favorable target window for anti-CD81 binders in AML, with lower off-target engagement on normal bone marrow progenitors.

accessibility_modulation.change: Surfaceome mass spectrometry (TS-MAP/SURFME) identifies CD81 as enriched on the surface of high-grade versus low-grade glioma, with divergent normalized relative abundance between the two tumor grades.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 is more accessible on high-grade glioma surfaces than on low-grade glioma, making it a candidate target enriched in the most aggressive brain tumor state; binders may discriminate tumor grade.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: GBM cell lines under normoxic conditions
accessibility_modulation.modulating_state: GBM cell lines under hypoxic stress (tumor microenvironment-mimicking conditions)
accessibility_modulation.change: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome; CD81 is validated as a surface candidate present under hypoxic conditions, implying modulation of its surface abundance by oxygen levels.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 surface accessibility in GBM may increase or be sustained under hypoxia, the dominant microenvironment of solid tumors; anti-CD81 binders or ADCs targeting GBM would encounter CD81 in the hypoxic tumor context.

accessibility_modulation.baseline_context: Wild-type HepG2 hepatoma cells with endogenous CD81 expression levels
accessibility_modulation.change: Endogenous CD81 is absent from the surface of wild-type HepG2 cells; forced overexpression drives high CD81 surface expression, indicating that surface trafficking is gated by expression level in this hepatoma line.
accessibility_modulation.accessibility_implication: CD81 surface accessibility in hepatoma cells is expression-level-dependent rather than constitutive; in low-CD81-expressing tumor contexts, surface target density may be insufficient for therapeutic engagement without prior characterization.

structure.source: AlphaFold DB (STUB — fetchers not yet built)

co_receptor_requirements.rationale: CD81 chaperones CD19 to the cell surface (~3-fold enhancement in HEK293T co-transfection assay), but CD81 itself traffics independently to the plasma membrane across many cell types without requiring CD19. The dependency is unidirectional; CD81 surface residency is not contingent on CD19 co-expression.

restricted_subdomain.rationale: Tetraspanins including CD81 are known to organize into tetraspanin-enriched microdomains (TEMs/rafts), but the ledger contains no direct evidence that this restricts systemic binder access. Surface detection by flow cytometry and biotinylation on intact cells is consistent with general plasma-membrane accessibility.

ecd_size_assessment.rationale: CD81 has two extracellular loops: a short ECL1 (~30 residues, aa 34–63) and a large extracellular loop LEL/ECL2 (~88 residues, aa 113–201). Although the LEL harbors the structured mushroom-shaped domain with the known HCV E2 and antibody binding site, the overall ECD footprint is limited compared to single-pass receptors, constraining epitope choice for binders.

epitope_masking.rationale: CD81 associates with multiple partners (CD19, CD9, CD63) within tetraspanin-enriched microdomains; partner interactions can occlude regions of the LEL. The LEL adopts a compact, disulfide-constrained mushroom fold that limits accessible epitope surface. These masking effects are inferred from structural and trafficking data rather than direct antibody competition mapping.

evidence.claim: TAPA-1 (CD81) is described as a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines, establishing broad surface accessibility across the human cell-line landscape as assessed by immunoprecipitation with an antiproliferative antibody.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 (the target of an antiproliferative antibody) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81/TAPA-1 belongs to a family of cell surface proteins all containing four transmembrane domains, confirming the tetraspanin 4-TM topology that defines two extracellular loops (one small, one large) accessible at the cell surface.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 is a member of an evolutionarily related family of cell surface proteins all of which contain four transmembrane domains.

evidence.claim: Topology of CD81/TAPA-1 was experimentally determined by proteolysis of in vitro translated protein embedded in microsomal membranes, providing direct biochemical evidence for the orientation of the transmembrane segments and extracellular loops.

cell_context.material_kind_other_label: in vitro translated protein in microsomal membranes

spans.quote: A model is proposed for topology of TAPA-1 based on proteolysis studies in the in vitro translated protein embedded into microsomal membranes.

evidence.claim: Proteolysis topology assay on microsome-embedded CD81 demonstrates that the amino and carboxyl termini are cytoplasmic and the two hydrophilic regions are extracellular — consistent with a 4-TM tetraspanin topology with two exposed extracellular domains (small loop ECL1 aa 34–63 and large loop ECL2 aa 113–201).

cell_context.material_kind_other_label: in vitro translated protein in microsomal membranes

spans.quote: This analysis predicts that the amino and the carboxyl termini of the molecule are cytoplasmic and that the two hydrophilic regions of the molecule are extracellular.

evidence.claim: The antigenic epitope of human CD81/TAPA-1 recognized by the antiproliferative antibody is localized within a subregion of the large extracellular domain (LEL, ECL2), confirming that this domain is surface-exposed and accessible to antibodies on intact cells.

spans.quote: The antigenic epitope of the human TAPA-1 is contained within a subregion of the second extracellular domain of the protein.

evidence.claim: Immunoprecipitation with an antiproliferative antibody identified a 26 kDa cell surface protein TAPA-1 (CD81) in the original discovery paper, providing early direct biochemical evidence for surface accessibility in an intact-cell immunoprecipitation assay.

spans.quote: Immunoprecipitation studies revealed that the antibody reacted with a 26-kilodalton cell surface protein (TAPA-1).

evidence.claim: Amino acid sequence analysis of CD81/TAPA-1 predicts a highly hydrophobic protein with four putative transmembrane domains and a potential N-myristoylation site, consistent with the 4-TM tetraspanin topology placing the two loops extracellularly.

spans.quote: Analysis of the deduced amino acid sequence indicated that the protein is highly hydrophobic and that it contains four putative transmembrane domains and a potential N-myristoylation site.

evidence.claim: The crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular loop domain (CD81-LEL) has been solved and used for Rosetta docking of HCV E2 epitope peptides, confirming that the LEL constitutes a structurally defined extracellular domain amenable to ligand and antibody engagement at the cell surface.

spans.quote: Because Ser 528 in epitope III is interchangeable with asparagine or threonine in the natural HCV variants, we tested Asn 528 - and Thr 528 -substituted peptides, assuming they take the same conformation displayed on the E2 structure, to see if they could dock onto the crystal structure of the human CD81 large extracellular domain (CD81-LEL) by using the Rosetta FastRelax protocols ( Fig. 5 ).

evidence.claim: HPA database annotates CD81 subcellular localization as plasma membrane and cytosol, providing a curated database vote for cell-surface accessibility.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Plasma membrane;Cytosol.

evidence.claim: HPA flags CD81 as plasma-membrane accessible based on IHC evidence, supporting surface expression classification.

spans.quote: HPA flags this gene as plasma-membrane accessible.

evidence.claim: HepG2 cells engineered to overexpress human CD81 were validated by live-cell flow cytometry for surface expression, using non-permeabilized intact cells to confirm CD81 is accessible at the plasma membrane.

spans.quote: The resulting cells were validated by flow cytometry for surface expression of CD81 ( Figure 1a ).

evidence.claim: Live-cell flow cytometry demonstrates that wild-type HepG2 cells lack detectable surface CD81 (used as a negative/knockout-equivalent control), whereas HepG2 cells stably overexpressing human CD81 show high surface CD81 signal — providing an isotype-like negative baseline and a strong positive control for antibody specificity in this assay context.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression were measured in AML patient bone marrow samples using live blasts (green) vs. hematogones (purple), directly quantifying surface-accessible CD81 on primary human AML cells without permeabilization.

spans.quote: Fig. 1 High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 mRNA expression correlates positively with cell surface CD81 abundance measured by flow cytometry across an AML patient cohort (r²=0.51), validating flow cytometry as the surface quantification method and confirming that RNA-level differences are reflected in plasma-membrane abundance.

spans.quote: In order to independently validate this finding, we applied the positive correlation found in our cohort between CD81 mRNA and cell surface abundance determined by flow cytometry (FCM) (Fig. 1c ) on two independent cohorts of AML.

evidence.claim: Representative flow cytometry histograms from AML bone marrow at diagnosis show CD81 surface expression on blasts (green) compared to hematogones (purple) in a cohort context; figure caption confirms intact-cell, non-permeabilized surface staining method.

spans.quote: High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: Violin plots summarizing CD81 cell surface expression by flow cytometry across the total AML study group (n=290) and comparison between CBF (n=26) and non-CBF (n=226) AML subtypes, confirming large-scale surface CD81 quantification on primary human leukemic cells.

spans.quote: The violin plot in the right panel summarizes low vs . high CD81 in the total AML study group (n = 290). b Violin plot comparing CD81 cell surface expression in CBF vs . non-CBF AML (n = 26, n = 226) and c scatterplot correlating CD81 z-score normalized mRNA vs .

evidence.claim: Flow cytometric analysis of live HEK293T wild-type cells and EVs derived from them confirmed surface CD81 (along with CD9 and CD63) on both intact cells and intact extracellular vesicles, establishing surface accessibility in a non-permeabilized intact-cell / intact-EV assay.

spans.quote: Flow cytometric analysis showed that HEK293T WT cells and WT‐EVs were positive for CD9, CD63, and CD81 on their surface (Figure 2B,C ).

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry was used to validate tetraspanin (CD9, CD63, CD81) surface expression on both cells and EVs following single-cell CRISPR knockout of each tetraspanin, confirming method specificity and providing KO-based negative controls for the surface detection assay.

spans.quote: Flow cytometry validation of tetraspanins surface expression on the cell and the EVs after single‐cell knockout of tetraspanins.

evidence.claim: In HEK293T cells, co-transfection with CD81 produces a ~3-fold increase in cell surface staining of a CD19-APEX2 fusion construct vs. CD19-APEX2 alone (measured by cell-surface staining assay on non-permeabilized cells), confirming that CD81 chaperones CD19 to the plasma membrane and that CD81 itself is surface-resident in this functional trafficking assay.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: Immunogold electron microscopy (iEM) on intact extracellular vesicles was used to visualize and quantify surface CD9 and CD81, confirming that CD81 is surface-exposed on EV membranes without permeabilization, using nanometer-resolution EM labeling.

spans.quote: To further characterize the EVs derived from cell lines, we performed immunogold EM (iEM) to visualize and quantify the surface expression of CD9 and CD81.

evidence.claim: Surface epitope detection on small EVs was performed using the MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec) by flow cytometry, simultaneously detecting 37 surface markers including CD81 (along with CD9, CD63 and others), with two isotype-control bead populations included. This establishes a bead-based flow cytometry method_family for EV surface CD81 detection with built-in isotype controls.

spans.quote: Isolated sEVs were characterized by flow cytometry using the MACSPlex Exosome Kit (Miltenyi Biotec, Bergisch Gladbach, Germany), which was developed for the simultaneous detection of 37 surface epitopes (CD1c, CD2, CD3, CD4, CD8, CD9, CD11c, CD14, CD19, CD20, CD24, CD25, CD29, CD31, CD40, CD41b, CD42a, CD44, CD45, CD49e, CD56, CD62P, CD63, CD69, CD81, CD86, CD105, CD133, CD142, CD146, CD209, CD326, HLA-ABC, HLA-DRDPDQ, MCSP, ROR1 and SSEA-4) that are known to be present on different exosomes and two isotype control beads.

evidence.claim: Surface biotinylation was performed on live cells at pH 8 using EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-biotin (1 mg/mL, 30 min), with glycine quench, to label and subsequently purify the intact-cell surfaceome. This membrane-impermeable reagent ensures only plasma-membrane-exposed proteins are labeled.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: U3034MG, U87MG, U3017MG, U3047MG (glioma cell lines)

spans.quote: Cells were washed with PBS (with MgCl 2 and CaCl 2 Mg/Ca-PBS) at pH 8 prior to incubation with a 1 mg/mL solution of membrane-impermeable biotin (EZ-Link Sulfo-N-hydroxysuccinimide-SS-biotin, Thermo Scientific, 21331) for 30 min, rinsed, incubated with 0.1 M glycine for 10 min to stop the reaction, and rinsed again prior to processing of surface biotinylated proteins (surfaceome).

evidence.claim: EV surface proteins were biotinylated using EZ-Link Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (10 mM, Cat No. 21331, ThermoFisher) in PBS pH 7.4 applied to EV pellets after ultracentrifugation, establishing surface-labeling method for mass spectrometry and WB validation of EV surface proteins including CD81.

spans.quote: Following the first ultracentrifugation steps for mEVs and sEVs at 16,500 g and 100,000 g, respectively, pellets were resuspended in 10 mM EZ-Link™ Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin (Cat No. 21331, ThermoFisher Scientific, Mississauga, ON, Canada) in PBS, pH 7.4.

evidence.claim: Proteomics of EV surface proteins was achieved by labeling surface-accessible proteins with Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin followed by enrichment and two-arm analysis (global proteomics + surface-enriched proteomics), validating surface accessibility of proteins including CD81 on EVs.

spans.quote: Proteomics samples were then analyzed using two approaches based on (1) global proteomic analysis and (2) enrichment of EV surface proteins by labeling surface-accessible proteins with a Sulfo-NHS-SS-Biotin reagent.

evidence.claim: Mass spectrometry after proximity labeling identified CD81 as one of nine proteins enriched by surface biotinylation approaches on live cells, confirming cell-surface accessibility of CD81 by an orthogonal MS-based surfaceome method in a study anchored by GCPII pulldown.

spans.quote: MS analysis identified GCPII as the most enriched protein, along with eight additional proteins previously detected in proximity labeling experiments: CD81 (CD81 antigen), CD9 (CD9 antigen), GPM6A (neuronal membrane glycoprotein M6-a), ITGB1 (integrin β1), MYADM (myeloid-associated differentiation marker), PROCR (endothelial protein C receptor), PTGFRN (prostaglandin F2 receptor negative regulator), and THY1 (Thy-1 membrane glycoprotein) (Fig. 3 B).

evidence.claim: CD81 exhibited >2-fold enrichment in surface biotinylation MS experiments and was detected by at least two independent biotinylation strategies, confirming high-confidence surface accessibility and methodological reproducibility across multiple surface-capture approaches.

spans.quote: Eight proteins exhibiting > 2-fold enrichment and identified previously by at least two biotinylation strategies are marked in blue (CD81, CD9, GPM6A, ITGB1, MYADM, PROCR, PTGFRN, and THY1).

evidence.claim: Following surface biotinylation and cell lysis, biotin-labeled surface proteins were enriched on streptavidin beads and then analyzed by Western blot, providing a paired surface-biotinylation + WB validation step confirming surface identity of enriched proteins including CD81.

spans.quote: Following cell lysis and protein enrichment on streptavidin beads, we performed Western blot analysis of the resulting enriched proteins.

evidence.claim: Biotin-labeled protein eluants from streptavidin-bead enrichment were analyzed by both Western blotting and mass spectrometry in parallel, constituting a paired WB + MS surface validation workflow for surface-biotinylation experiments in which CD81 was identified.

spans.quote: Protein eluants after streptavidin-bead enrichment were analyzed by Western blotting and mass spectrometry.

evidence.claim: Among 456 proteins detected by MS in cells and EVs, 136 (30%) including CD81 were annotated by UniProt as membrane/cell-surface proteins, with CD81 carrying at least one transmembrane domain, confirming the topology annotation for CD81 in this proteomics dataset.

spans.quote: Uniprot categorized 136 of the 456 proteins (29.82%) proteins as being present in membranes, on the cell membrane, or on the cell surface; 65 of them, including CD81, CD9, and CD63, have at least one (and up to 12) transmembrane domains ( Dataset S15 ).

evidence.claim: CD81 was used as an EV marker loading control in Western blot of whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations alongside GAPDH and DPP4-Myc, confirming that CD81 is present in EV surface fractions as detected by WB (whole-cell WB without fractionation context — non-surface-specific).

spans.quote: We first validated the production of DPP4-Myc fusion protein, along with loading control GAPDH and EV marker CD81, in HeLa whole-cell lysates, total EVs, and EV surface protein preparations.

evidence.claim: Surface proteins on cells and EVs were isolated for mass spectrometry and Western blot analysis using a surface protein isolation protocol, establishing the method_family (surface-protein isolation + MS + WB) for subsequent CD81 surface detection in this study.

spans.quote: Surface proteins on cells and EVs were isolated for mass spectrometry (MS) and western blot analysis.

evidence.claim: Western blot antibody for CD81 used in EV characterization studies: Santa Cruz #SC-166029 at 1:500 dilution, applied overnight at 4°C. Antibody identity and catalog number documented for methodological reproducibility; used in EV fractionation WB to confirm CD81 as an EV surface marker.

spans.quote: Membranes were then incubated with the following primary antibodies overnight at 4°C: CD63 (1:1000, EMD Millipore #CBL553), CD81 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐166029), CD9 (1:500, Cell Signaling #D801A), Calnexin (1:1000, Cell signaling #2433S), Annexin 1 (1:500, Santa Cruz #SC‐12740), HSP90B1 (1:1000, Cell Signaling #7411), and Streptavidin HRP (1:10,000, Cell Signaling #3999) all diluted in 5% BSA.

evidence.claim: Anti-CD81 antibody (rabbit, Abcam ab219209, 1:200) is specified for use in immunofluorescence or flow-cytometry-adjacent applications in this GBM tissue study, documenting the antibody clone and dilution for methodological validation of CD81 surface detection.

spans.quote: Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-75459), mouse anti-CD63 (1/100, Abcam, ab8219), mouse anti-CD45-PE (1/25, BD Biosciences, 555483), rabbit anti-CD68 (1/800, Cell Signaling Technology, D4B9C), mouse anti-CD31 (1/100, Dako, JC70A), mouse anti-CD44 (1/100, Dako, DF1485), mouse anti-CD14-PE (1/25, BioLegend), rabbit anti-BCAN (1/200, Abcam, ab111719), rabbit anti-CD81 (1/200, Abcam, ab219209), rabbit anti-TF (kindly provided by Wolfram Ruf, Center for Thrombosis and Hemostasis, University of Mainz, Mainz, Germany), or rabbit anti-MCT2 (1/100, Thermo Fisher Scientific, PA5-112712).

evidence.claim: HPA database annotation places CD81 at dual subcellular locations: plasma membrane and cytosol, supporting cell-surface presence with a cytosolic pool.

spans.quote: HPA subcellular locations: Plasma membrane;Cytosol.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) protein is broadly expressed across a diverse group of human cell lines including hematolymphoid, neuroectodermal, and mesenchymal lineages, establishing wide baseline tissue/cell-type expression breadth.

spans.quote: A diverse group of human cell lines, including hematolymphoid, neuroectodermal, and mesenchymal cells, expressed the TAPA-1 protein.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is expressed in lymphoid cell lines, particularly large cell lymphoma-derived lines, where it mediates antiproliferative antibody sensitivity.

spans.quote: Many of the lymphoid cell lines, in particular those derived from large cell lymphomas, were susceptible to the antiproliferative effects of the antibody.

evidence.claim: CD81 (TAPA-1) is a 26-kDa cell-surface protein expressed on most human cell lines, establishing broad baseline surface expression across human cell types.

spans.quote: TAPA-1 (the target of an antiproliferative antibody) is a 26-kDa cell surface protein expressed on most human cell lines.

evidence.claim: CD81 shows cytoplasmic and membranous expression in germ cells within seminiferous tubules of testicular tissue, serving as an established positive control for CD81 localization in this tissue compartment.

spans.quote: CD81: Cytoplasmic and membranous expression of the germ cells within the seminiferous tubules was considered as positive control ( Fig. 1 c1).

evidence.claim: CD81, as an exosomal surface marker, is detected by IHC in ovarian cancer (OvCa) tissue, where exosomal CD81 is examined in the context of premetastatic niche formation.

spans.quote: We have performed IHC analysis for exosomal surface marker CD81 in OvCa tissues as exosomes participate in preparing a premetastatic niche (PMN) for the migrating cancer cells at the secondary site and promote metastasis [ 32 ].

evidence.claim: CD81 co-transfection in HEK293T cells drives ~3-fold increase in CD19 cell-surface delivery, confirming that CD81 itself traffics to the cell surface and acts as a chaperone for CD19 surface localization; CD81 thus achieves functional plasma membrane presence in this heterologous expression system.

spans.quote: Properly folded CD19 is trafficked to the cell surface by forming a complex with the tetraspanin CD81. 29 As an initial test for the functional integrity of our fusion construct, we used a trafficking assay in HEK293T cells to assess the ability of CD81 to enhance the delivery of CD19-APEX2 to the cell surface. 30 , 31 Our CD19-APEX2 protein matches the response of wild-type CD19, showing a 3-fold increase in cell surface staining when co-transfected with CD81 ( Figures S1B and S1C ), confirming that the fusion protein was properly transported to the cell surface and that its interaction with

evidence.claim: CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 hepatoma cells but is induced by overexpression of hCD81, indicating that endogenous CD81 is not trafficked to the surface in this hepatoma line under basal conditions — surface presence is expression-level dependent.

spans.quote: As previously reported [ 12 ], CD81 surface expression is absent on wild-type HepG2 cells (grey histogram), whereas HepG2 cells overexpressing hCD81 show high CD81 surface expression (red histogram).

evidence.claim: High CD81 surface expression on AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis is associated with relapse and adverse clinical outcomes in non-core-binding factor (non-CBF) AML, establishing CD81 as a disease-state-linked surface marker in AML.

spans.quote: Analysis of retrospective patient cohorts revealed that high CD81 surface expression is associated with relapse and adverse clinical outcomes in non-core-binding factor AML.

evidence.claim: CD81 is enriched within leukemia stem cell (LSC)-containing subpopulations of AML bone marrow, where its expression supports LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy — indicating that high CD81 surface expression is a feature of the stem-like, therapy-resistant AML cell state.

spans.quote: Notably, CD81 was enriched within LSC-containing subpopulations, where its expression supported LSC maintenance and resistance to chemotherapy.

evidence.claim: Flow cytometry histograms confirm CD81 surface expression on AML bone marrow blasts at diagnosis, with blasts showing higher CD81 surface levels compared to normal hematogones in the same bone marrow sample.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts vs hematogones

cell_context.cell_type: AML blasts; hematogones (normal B cell precursors)

spans.quote: Fig. 1 High CD81 surface expression associates with relapse and adverse outcome in non-CBF AML. a Shown are representative flow cytometry histograms of CD81 surface expression measured in AML bone marrow taken at diagnosis with blasts (green) and corresponding hematogones (purple).

evidence.claim: CD81 high cell surface expression at AML diagnosis is validated as a poor prognostic marker in an extended cohort of 252 diagnostic and 38 relapsed AML samples, confirming and elaborating earlier findings in 134 de novo AML patients.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML bone marrow blasts (diagnostic and relapsed samples, n=290)

spans.quote: Previously, we have associated high CD81 cell surface expression at diagnosis with poor prognosis in a cohort of 134 de novo AML. 32 These initial findings are now validated and elaborated using an extended cohort of 252 diagnostic and 38 relapsed AML samples (CONSORT diagram Supplementary Fig. 1 ).

evidence.claim: CBF-AML blasts (favorable prognosis subtype, n=26) show significantly lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML blasts (n=226), demonstrating disease-subtype-dependent modulation of CD81 surface levels in AML.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML blasts — CBF vs non-CBF subtypes

spans.quote: In this study, we show that core-binding factor (CBF) AML (n = 26), which have well-known favorable prognosis, 3 have lower CD81 cell surface expression compared to non-CBF AML (Fig. 1b , Supplementary Fig. 3a ).

evidence.claim: CBF-AML have significantly lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML, confirmed in the Leucegene cohort and Beat AML dataset, corroborating flow cytometry surface protein findings at the transcript level.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: AML patient samples — CBF vs non-CBF (Leucegene and Beat AML cohorts)

spans.quote: Consistent with our results, CBF-AML have significant lower CD81 mRNA expression compared to non-CBF AML shown for the Leucegene cohort 33 and the Beat AML dataset 34 (Fig. 1d ).

evidence.claim: High CD81 mRNA expression adversely affects overall survival in non-CBF AML (HR=1.74) and NPM1-mutant AML (HR=4.18) in the Beat AML dataset, with NPM1-mutant AML showing a trend toward lower CD81 expression; CD81 expression varies across AML molecular subtypes.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: non-CBF AML and NPM1-mutant AML (Beat AML dataset)

spans.quote: Despite the exclusion of good prognosis CBF-AML and that CD81 expression was not different among other major AML subtypes (Supplementary Fig. 4 ), high diagnostic CD81 mRNA expression adversely affected overall survival (OS) in non-CBF AML and in NPM1- mutant AML using the Beat AML dataset (Fig. 1e ; non-CBF AML: HR [CI] = 1.74 [0.97–3.12]; NPM1 -mutant AML: HR [CI] = 4.18 [1.42–12.36]). 35 , 36 Of note, AML with NPM1 mutation showed lower CD81 expression, although this did not reach statistical significance

evidence.claim: CD81 shows divergent normalized relative surface abundance between high-grade and low-grade brain tumors (gliomas) by tumor-surface mapping, identifying CD81 as a candidate surface target enriched in high-grade glioma.

assay_context.cell_type_or_line: high-grade vs low-grade glioma tumor specimens

spans.quote: We selected EGFR, BCAN, CD81, TF, and MCT2 for further studies based on their divergent, normalized relative abundances between high- and low-grade tumors according to TS-MAP/SURFME data ( Fig. 3 F ).

evidence.claim: Hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM cell surface proteome (surface-endocytome), with CD81 identified as a validated surface candidate in glioblastoma cell lines under hypoxic stress conditions — suggesting accessibility modulation by tumor microenvironment oxygen levels.

spans.quote: We reveal how hypoxic stress profoundly reshapes the GBM surface-endocytome, and validate several potential ADC target candidates; CXADR, CD47, CD81, BSG, and FXYD6.

confidence_reasoning: A1 evidence grade is direct_multi_method with zero contradicting evidence entries. Multiple orthogonal direct methods (live-cell flow cytometry on primary patient samples with CRISPR-KO controls, surface biotinylation MS with >2-fold enrichment across ≥2 strategies, immunogold EM on intact EVs, and biochemical topology proteolysis) converge on the same conclusion. A2 introduces moderate state-dependence (disease-upregulated in AML and high-grade glioma; near-absent on wild-type HepG2) but no dominant secreted/shed form or topology contradiction. Integrated confidence is therefore high, tracking A1's grade with a note that expression-level variation across contexts creates moderate state-dependence for target-density estimates.
